questions.q: What are the five classic stages of a simple RISC pipeline?
What is the main purpose of branch prediction?
questions.a: To guess the next control-flow path early and keep the pipeline full.

questions.q: What is the cache line size commonly used on modern x86 CPUs?
What kind of data does a CPU register hold compared with RAM?
questions.a: The smallest, fastest working set used directly by instructions.

questions.q: Why does an out-of-order CPU improve throughput?
questions.a: It keeps useful work moving while waiting on stalls by reordering independent instructions.

questions.q: Why is a branch misprediction expensive?
questions.a: The CPU must discard speculative work and refill the pipeline from the correct path.

questions.q: What problem does cache locality solve for performance?
questions.a: It reduces slow memory accesses by reusing data already near the CPU.

questions.q: Why can vectorized code be faster than scalar code?
questions.a: One instruction operates on many values at once, increasing work per cycle.

questions.q: Why do modern CPUs use deep pipelines and wide issue width together?
questions.a: Deep pipelines raise clock potential, while wide issue executes more independent work per cycle.
Two threads update different variables that happen to share the same cache line.

questions.q: When would you prefer a higher clock speed over more cores?
questions.a: When the workload is mostly single-threaded, latency-sensitive, or limited by serial sections.

questions.q: Why can aggressive speculative execution be a security risk?
questions.a: Speculation can leak microarchitectural state even when the speculative path is later discarded.

questions.q: How do you decide between optimizing branch prediction and data layout?
questions.a: Profile first; if stalls come from control flow, improve predictability, otherwise improve data locality.

questions.q: Why can adding more execution units stop helping after a point?
questions.a: Independent work, memory bandwidth, or front-end fetch/decode can become the bottleneck.

questions.q: When is manual vectorization worth the cost?
questions.a: When a hot loop is stable, arithmetic-heavy, and the speedup is large enough to justify complexity.
It can improve utilization, but threads also compete for shared core resources and may hurt isolation.
